title: Entanglement generation between field modes mediated by a fluctuating conducting wall
authors: Luca Giovanni Cammarata, Tommaso Fazio, Roberto Passante, Lucia Rizzuto
abstract: We consider a movable conducting plate of finite mass, between two fixed ones, whose mechanical degrees of freedom are treated quantum-mechanically and bound to its equilibrium position by a harmonic potential. The movable wall is thus subjected to quantum fluctuations of its position. This creates a system of two sub-cavities separated by the movable fluctuating plate, and two massless one-dimensional scalar fields, one in each sub-cavity. This system is described by an appropriate generalization of the Law Hamiltonian. The presence of the movable wall yields an effective plate-fields interaction, as well as an effective interaction between the field modes. We obtain, at the second order in perturbation theory, the ground state of the interacting system and the reduced density operator of the fields in each sub-cavity by tracing out the wall's degrees of freedom. We calculate the entanglement between two field modes, one in each cavity, by evaluating analytically the negativity; we then evaluate numerically also the total multimode negativity. Our results show that in both cases the fields in the two sub-cavities are entangled, in contrast to the case in which the wall is fixed in space. We discuss the amount of the field entanglement present as a function of relevant physical parameters of the system such as the mass and oscillation frequency of the movable wall, its distance from the fixed walls and the frequencies of the field modes considered.
summary: The paper models two field cavities separated by a movable, quantum mechanically fluctuating wall. By treating the system's dynamics using perturbation theory and calculating entanglement via negativity, the authors demonstrate that vacuum fluctuations of the wall generate significant entanglement between the fields in the two separate cavities. This highlights how mechanical degrees of freedom can mediate non-local quantum correlations.
main_problem: The study investigates how quantum entanglement can be generated between field modes confined in two separate sub-cavities when these cavities are separated by a movable, fluctuating conducting wall.
main_result: Quantum position fluctuations of the movable wall generate measurable entanglement between the field modes in opposite sub-cavities, an effect absent if the wall were fixed.
method: The analysis employs second-order perturbation theory to find the interacting ground state and calculates entanglement using the negativity measure on the reduced density operator by tracing out the wall's degrees of freedom.
model_or_system: The system consists of two massless 1D scalar fields confined in sub-cavities separated by a movable conducting plate. The wall's mechanical motion is quantized via a harmonic potential, leading to an effective field-wall interaction Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Negativity (N), which quantifies the entanglement between the two field modes; total multi-mode negativity.
important_parameters: Mass ($M$) and oscillation frequency ($\omega_0$) of the movable wall, sub-cavity lengths ($l_1, l_2$), and the frequencies of the considered field modes ($\omega_k, \omega_j$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is performed using perturbation theory up to second order in the interaction Hamiltonian; the use of negativity is necessary because the reduced state is generally mixed.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the dependence of entanglement (negativity) on the wall's equilibrium position, mode frequencies, and the number of modes considered, showing maximization under specific resonance conditions.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the Hamiltonian describing the coupled system, derives the reduced density operator by tracing out the wall, calculates the negativity analytically for two modes, and then extends this analysis numerically to include multiple field modes while discussing parameter dependencies.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses quantum light-matter interaction in a cavity setting (cavity QED analogue), showing how mechanical fluctuations can act as an effective mediator for generating non-classical correlations between distant quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Pictorial representation of a cavity divided into two sub-cavities by a fluctuating conducting wall with equilibrium position between the two external walls.
FIG. 2: The negativity (18) as a function of x, the wall’s equilibrium position in units of L0.
FIG. 3: The negativity (18) as a function of ω1 ≡ωk and ω2 ≡ωj, in units of ω0, for the symmetric configuration l1 = l2 = L0.
FIG. 4: Negativity in Eq. (19) as a function of ω, in units of ω0, for the symmetric configuration l1 = l2 = L0.
FIG. 5: 3D histogram of the negativity in Eq. (18), quantifying the entanglement between pairs of modes k and j, in terms of the dimensionless parameter ℏ/(64ML2 0ω0).
